title.zh: 卷五十 孝明八王列傳
title.en: Volume 50: Biographies of Eight Princes of Xiaoming
title.raw: 卷五十 孝明八王列傳 Volume 50: Biographies of Eight Princes of Xiaoming

sentences.zh: 千乘哀王建

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Qiansheng, Jian.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Ai of Qiansheng, Liu Jian.

sentences.zh: 千乘哀王建，永平三年封。

translations.literal: Prince Ai of Qiansheng, Jian, in the third year of Yongping was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jian, posthumously honored as Prince Ai of Qiansheng, received his fief in the third year of Yongping (60 CE).

sentences.zh: 明年薨。

translations.literal: The next year he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died the following year.

sentences.zh: 年少無子，國除。

translations.literal: Young in years, without a son, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He was still young and left no heir, so the kingdom was abolished.

sentences.zh: 陳敬王羨

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Chen, Xian.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jing of Chen, Liu Xian.

sentences.zh: 陳敬王羨，永平三年封廣平王。

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Chen, Xian, in the third year of Yongping was enfeoffed as Prince of Guangping.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xian, later honored as Prince Jing of Chen, was first made Prince of Guangping in the third year of Yongping (60 CE).

sentences.zh: 建初三年，有司奏遣羨與鉅鹿王恭、樂成王黨俱就國。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianchu, the responsible officials memorialized to send Xian together with Prince Gong of Julu and Prince Dang of Lecheng all to their states.
translations.idiomatic: In 78 CE officials asked that Liu Xian be sent to his fief together with the Princes of Julu and Lecheng so that all would take up residence in their kingdoms.

sentences.zh: 肅宗性篤愛，不忍與諸王乖離，遂皆留京師。

translations.literal: Suzong by nature was deeply loving and could not bear to be separated from the various princes, and so all were kept at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang was deeply attached to his brothers and could not bear to part with them, so every prince was allowed to remain at the capital.

sentences.zh: 明年，案輿地圖，令諸國戶口皆等，租入歲各八千萬。

translations.literal: The next year, examining the map of territories, he ordered the households of the various states all to be equal, rent income each year eight thousand ten-thousands.
translations.idiomatic: The next year the court consulted the territorial register and equalized the registered households of each princedom so that annual revenue from rents was set at eighty million cash for every kingdom.

sentences.zh: 羨博涉經書，有威嚴，與諸儒講論於白虎殿。

translations.literal: Xian broadly perused the classics and histories, had awe-inspiring dignity, and with various scholars discussed and debated in the White Tiger Hall.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xian read widely in the canon, carried himself with commanding presence, and joined other scholars in disputation in the White Tiger Hall.

sentences.zh: 注[一]廣平，縣，故城在今洺州永年縣北。

translations.literal: Comment one: Guangping, a county; the old walled town is north of Yongnian County in modern Mingzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Guangping was a county; its old city lay north of present-day Yongnian in Hebei.

sentences.zh: 注[二]西平，縣，屬汝南郡也。

translations.literal: Comment two: Xiping, a county, belonged to Runan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Xiping County lay within Runan commandery.

sentences.zh: 鈞立，多不法，遂行天子大射禮。

translations.literal: When Jun was established, there were many unlawful acts, and he then performed the Son of Heaven's grand archery ritual.
translations.idiomatic: Once Liu Jun succeeded, his conduct was often lawless; he even staged the grand archery ceremony reserved for the emperor.

sentences.zh: [一]性隱賊，喜文法，國相二千石不與相得者，輒陰中之。

translations.literal: [One] His nature was secretive and vicious; he delighted in statutes and precedents, and whenever a kingdom chancellor at the two-thousand-dan rank did not get along with him, he would secretly strike at them.
translations.idiomatic: He was by nature devious and cruel, relished legalistic wrangling, and whenever a chancellor of two-thousand-dan rank crossed him, he would find a covert way to ruin the man.

sentences.zh: 憎怨敬王夫人李儀等，永元十一年，遂使客隗久[二]殺儀家屬。

translations.literal: He bore hatred toward the consort of Prince Jing, Lady Li Yi and others; in the eleventh year of Yongyuan he then had his retainer Wei Jiu kill Yi's family members.
translations.idiomatic: Nursing a grudge against Prince Jing's lady Li Yi and her kin, in 99 CE he sent his agent Wei Jiu (or Wen) to murder her entire household.

sentences.zh: 吏捕得久，系長平獄。

translations.literal: Officials captured Jiu and bound him in Changping prison.
translations.idiomatic: The authorities seized Wei Jiu and threw him into the jail at Changping.

sentences.zh: [三]鈞欲斷絕辭語，復使結客篡殺久。

translations.literal: [Three] Jun wished to cut off testimony and again had retainers band together to abduct and kill Jiu.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jun tried to silence the witness and hired assassins to spring Jiu from custody and murder him.

sentences.zh: 事發覺，有司舉奏，鈞坐削西華、項、新陽三縣。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, the responsible officials memorialized; Jun was sentenced to stripping away the three counties Xihua, Xiang, and Xinyang.
translations.idiomatic: The plot came to light; after officials reported it, Liu Jun was punished by forfeiture of the three counties of Xihua, Xiang, and Xinyang.

sentences.zh: [四]十二年，封鈞六弟為列侯。

translations.literal: [Four] In the twelfth year, Jun's six younger brothers were enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 100 CE his six younger brothers were each made full marquises.

sentences.zh: [五]後鈞取掖庭出女李嬈為小妻，[六]復坐削圉、宜祿、扶溝三縣。

translations.literal: [Five] Later Jun took the palace woman Li Rao who had been released from the harem as a minor wife, [six] and again was sentenced to stripping away the three counties Yu, Yilu, and Fugou.
translations.idiomatic: He later took Lady Li Rao, a woman dismissed from the imperial harem, as a concubine and again lost the three counties of Yu, Yilu, and Fugou as a penalty.

sentences.zh: [七]永初七年，封敬王孫安國為耕亭侯。

translations.literal: [Seven] In the seventh year of Yongchu, Prince Jing's grandson Anguo was enfeoffed as Marquis of Gengting.
translations.idiomatic: In 113 CE Liu Anguo, a grandson of Prince Jing, was enfeoffed as Marquis of Gengting.

sentences.zh: 注[一]天子將祭，擇士而祭，謂之大射。

translations.literal: Comment one: When the Son of Heaven is about to sacrifice, he selects gentlemen to sacrifice to; this is called the grand archery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Before the emperor sacrifices, he selects officers for the ritual; that selection rite is the grand archery.

sentences.zh: 大射之禮，張三侯，虎侯、熊侯、豹侯，示服猛也，皆以其皮方制之。

translations.literal: In the rite of grand archery, three targets are set up: tiger target, bear target, leopard target, showing submission to fierceness; all are made square from their hides.
translations.idiomatic: The ceremony sets up three hides—the tiger, bear, and leopard targets—emblems of mastery over the fierce, each cut square from the animal's pelt.

sentences.zh: 樂用騶虞，九節。

translations.literal: The music uses Zouyu, nine sections.
translations.idiomatic: The musicians play "Zouyu" in nine sections.

sentences.zh: 謝承書曰「陳國戶曹史高慎諫國相曰：『諸侯射豕，天子射熊，八彝六樽，禮數不同。

translations.literal: Xie Cheng's book says: "The household-clerk Gao Shen of Chen remonstrated with the kingdom chancellor: 'Feudal lords shoot pig; the Son of Heaven shoots bear; eight yi vessels and six zun vessels—the ritual numbers are not the same.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng records that Gao Shen, a clerk in the Chen revenue office, warned his chancellor: "Marquises shoot at pigs; the emperor shoots at bears; eight sacrificial goblets versus six wine vessels—the scales of ritual differ."

sentences.zh: 昔季氏設朱干玉戚以舞大夏。

translations.literal: Formerly the Ji clan set up red shields and jade axes to dance the Great Xia.
translations.idiomatic: Long ago the Ji family danced the Great Xia with crimson shields and jade halberds.

sentences.zh: 左傳曰：「唯名與器，不可以假人。

translations.literal: The Zuo Commentary says: 'Only titles and ritual vessels may not be lent to others.
translations.idiomatic: The Zuo Tradition says, "Regalia and ritual insignia must never be loaned out."

sentences.zh: 」奢僭之漸，不可聽也。

translations.literal: ' The gradual advance of extravagance and usurpation cannot be listened to.
translations.idiomatic: To allow this slide toward luxury and presumption would be intolerable.

sentences.zh: 』於是諫爭不合，為王所非，坐司寇罪」也。

translations.literal: '" Thereupon remonstration did not accord, was faulted by the king, and he sat guilty of the Minister of Crime offense" also.
translations.idiomatic: His protests were ignored, the prince turned on him, and he was convicted on a charge handled by the Minister of Justice.

sentences.zh: 注[二]「久」或作「文」。

translations.literal: Comment two: "Jiu" is sometimes written "Wen".
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Some texts write the name as Wen instead of Jiu.

sentences.zh: 注[三]長平，縣，屬陳國。

translations.literal: Comment three: Changping, a county, belonged to the state of Chen.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Changping County lay within the princedom of Chen.

sentences.zh: 注[四]西華故城在今陳州□水縣西北。

translations.literal: Comment four: The old city of Xihua is northwest of Shangshui County in Chenzhou today (the received text has a lacuna before 水).
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The old seat of Xihua lay northwest of the Shangshui county seat in Chenzhou (one character missing in the received text).

sentences.zh: 項，今陳州項城縣也。

translations.literal: Xiang is today Xiangcheng County in Chenzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Xiang is present-day Xiangcheng in Henan.

sentences.zh: 新陽故城在今豫州真陽縣西南也。

translations.literal: The old city of Xinyang is southwest of Zhenyang County in Yuzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: The old town of Xinyang stood southwest of Zhenyang in southern Henan.

sentences.zh: 注[五]伏侯古今注曰「番為陽都鄉侯，千秋為新平侯，參為周亭侯，壽為樂陽亭侯，寶為博平侯，旦為高亭侯」也。

translations.literal: Comment five: The Fuhou ancient and modern commentary says: "Fan was Marquis of Yangdu village, Qianqiu was Marquis of Xinping, Can was Marquis of Zhouting, Shou was Marquis of Leyang village, Bao was Marquis of Boping, Dan was Marquis of Gaoting" also.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: The Fuhou commentary lists the six brothers' marquisates: Yangdu village, Xinping, Zhouting village, Leyang village, Boping, and Gaoting.

sentences.zh: 注[六]嬈音寧了反。

translations.literal: Comment six: Rao is read ning-liao fan.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6: The graph read rao is pronounced with the ning-liao fanqie gloss, an entering-tone reading.

sentences.zh: 注[七]圉、扶溝並屬陳留都。

translations.literal: Comment seven: Yu and Fugou both belonged to Chenliu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 7: Yu and Fugou both lay in Chenliu commandery; the commentary writes the graph for metropolis where other texts have the word for commandery.

sentences.zh: 宜祿屬汝南郡。

translations.literal: Yilu belonged to Runan commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Yilu lay in Runan commandery.

sentences.zh: 鈞立二十一年薨，子懷王竦嗣。

translations.literal: Jun, having been established twenty-one years, died; his son Prince Huai, Song, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty-one years on the throne Liu Jun died and was succeeded by his son Liu Song, posthumously Prince Huai.

sentences.zh: 立二年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Established two years, died, without a son, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He reigned only two years and left no heir, so the line lapsed.

sentences.zh: 承薨，子愍王寵嗣。

translations.literal: Cheng died; his son Prince Min, Chong, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Cheng died, his son Liu Chong succeeded as Prince Min.

sentences.zh: 熹平二年，國相師遷追奏前相魏愔與寵共祭天神，希幸非冀，罪至不道。

translations.literal: In the second year of Xiping, the kingdom chancellor Shi Qian retrospectively memorialized the former chancellor Wei Yin as having with Chong together sacrificed to Heaven's spirit, hoping for improper favor, guilt reaching lack of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: In 173 CE Chancellor Shi Qian charged his predecessor Wei Yin with having joined Liu Chong in illicit sacrifices to heaven, seeking unlawful blessings—a crime deemed unfilial and subversive.

sentences.zh: 有司奏遣使者案驗。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized to send envoys to investigate and verify.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry asked that imperial agents be dispatched to investigate.

sentences.zh: 是時新誅勃海王悝，[一]靈帝不忍復加法，詔檻車傳送愔、遷詣北寺詔獄，使中常侍王酺[二]與尚書令、侍御史雜考。

translations.literal: At this time having newly executed Prince Kui of Bohai, [one] Lingdi could not bear again to add punishment, ordered caged carts to transmit Yin and Qian to the Northern Office imperial prison, ordered Palace Attendant Wang Pu [two] together with the Director of the Secretariat and attendant censors jointly to examine.
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Bohai had just been forced to suicide; Emperor Ling shrank from another harsh sentence on a kinsman. He had Yin and Shi Qian carted to the Northern Office jail and ordered the eunuch Wang Pu (or Fu) to join the secretariat director and palace censors in a joint inquiry.

sentences.zh: 愔辭與王共祭黃老君，求長生福而已，無它冀幸。

translations.literal: Yin's statement: with the king together they sacrificed to the Yellow Lord Laozi, seeking long-life blessing only, without other hope.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Yin testified that he and the prince had merely offered cult to the Yellow Lord and Laozi for longevity, with no ulterior designs.

sentences.zh: 酺等奏愔職在匡正，而所為不端，遷誣告其王，罔以不道，皆誅死。

translations.literal: Pu and others memorialized that Yin, whose duty was to correct, yet conduct was not upright; Qian falsely accused his king, deceived with lack of the Way; all were executed to death.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Pu's panel ruled that Wei Yin had failed his duty as moral tutor, while Shi Qian had slandered his prince with capital charges; both were executed.

sentences.zh: 有詔赦寵不案。

translations.literal: There was an edict pardoning Chong, not investigating.
translations.idiomatic: An edict spared Liu Chong further prosecution.

sentences.zh: 注[一]靈帝熹平元年，悝被誣謀反自殺也。

translations.literal: Comment one: In the first year of Xiping of Lingdi, Kui was slandered plotting rebellion and committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: In 172 CE the Prince of Bohai was accused of rebellion and took his own life.

sentences.zh: 注[二]華嶠書及宦者傳諸本並作「甫」，此云「酺」，未詳孰是也。

translations.literal: Comment two: Hua Qiao's book and the eunuch biography various editions all write "Fu"; this writes "Pu"; it is not clear which is correct.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Hua Qiao and the Treatise on Eunuchs read the name as Fu; this passage has Pu—the correct form is uncertain.

sentences.zh: 寵善弩射，十發十中，中皆同處。

translations.literal: Chong was skilled at crossbow shooting, ten shots ten hits, the hits all at the same place.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chong was a master of the crossbow: ten shafts, ten bull's-eyes, every bolt clustering in the same spot.

sentences.zh: [一]中平中，黃巾賊起，郡縣皆□城走，寵有強弩數千張，出軍都亭。

translations.literal: [One] In Zhongping, when the Yellow Turban bandits arose, commanderies and counties all abandoned the cities and fled (one character missing in the received text); Chong had several thousand strong crossbows and went out with troops at the capital district pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: During the Zhongping era (184–189 CE) the Yellow Turbans rose; county seats everywhere emptied as officials fled. Chong mustered thousands of heavy crossbows and encamped his guard at the metropolitan pavilion.

sentences.zh: [二]國人素聞王善射，不敢反叛，故陳獨得完，百姓歸之者觿十余萬人。

translations.literal: [Two] The people of the state had long heard the king was skilled at shooting, did not dare rebel; therefore Chen alone was able to be intact, common people who returned to him were more than a hundred thousand.
translations.idiomatic: The populace knew their prince could shoot, so they never turned bandit; Chen alone stayed intact, and well over a hundred thousand refugees flocked to him.

sentences.zh: 及獻帝初，義兵起，寵率觿屯陽夏，[三]自稱輔漢大將軍。

translations.literal: When at the beginning of Emperor Xian the righteous troops arose, Chong led the masses and encamped at Yangxia, [three] calling himself Grand General Who Aids Han.
translations.idiomatic: At the start of Emperor Xian's reign he raised an army, camped at Yangxia, and styled himself Grand General in Aid of the Han.

sentences.zh: 國相會稽駱俊素有威恩，時天下饑荒，鄰郡人多歸就之，俊傾資賑贍，並得全活。

translations.literal: The kingdom chancellor Luo Jun of Kuaiji by nature had awesome kindness; at the time the realm was famine-stricken, people of neighboring commanderies many returned to him; Jun poured out resources to relieve and nourish, and together obtained preservation and life.
translations.idiomatic: His chancellor Luo Jun of Kuaiji was beloved and feared in equal measure. Famine gripped the empire, and neighbors poured into Chen; Luo Jun spent his own fortune on relief and kept them alive.

sentences.zh: 後袁術求糧於陳而俊拒絕之，術忿恚，遣客詐殺俊及寵，陳由是破敗。

translations.literal: Later Yuan Shu sought grain from Chen but Jun refused him; Shu was resentful and angry, sent retainers by deception to kill Jun and Chong; Chen from this was broken and defeated.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shu later demanded grain and Luo Jun rebuffed him. Shu sent assassins who murdered both the chancellor and the prince, and the kingdom of Chen collapsed.

sentences.zh: [四] 注[一]華嶠書曰：「寵射，其秘法以天覆地載，參連為奇。

translations.literal: [Four] Comment one: Hua Qiao's book says: "Chong's shooting, its secret method used heaven covering earth bearing, triple-link as odd.
translations.idiomatic: Hua Qiao records his technique: stances called "sky covering" and "earth bearing," with a triple-linked "odd" formation.

sentences.zh: 又有三微﹑三小。

translations.literal: Further there are three subtle and three small.
translations.idiomatic: There were also the "three subtleties" and the "three fines."

sentences.zh: 三微為經，三小為緯，經緯相將，萬勝之方，然要在機牙。」

translations.literal: The three subtle are warp, the three small are weft, warp and weft mutually assist, the method of ten thousand victories, yet the essential lies in the trigger mechanism."
translations.idiomatic: The subtleties formed the warp, the fines the weft; woven together they were the key to unfailing hits—but the real secret lay in the release of the trigger."

sentences.zh: 注[二]置軍營於國之都亭也。

translations.literal: Comment two: He placed military camp at the state's capital district pavilion.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: He quartered troops at the princedom's metropolitan pavilion.

sentences.zh: 注[三]縣名，屬淮陽國。

translations.literal: Comment three: A county name, belonged to Huaiyang state.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Yangxia was a county in the old kingdom of Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 夏音公雅反。

translations.literal: Xia is read gong-ya fan.
translations.idiomatic: In the place-name Yangxia the second syllable is read with the gong-ya fanqie gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[四]謝承書曰：「俊字孝遠，烏傷人。

translations.literal: Comment four: Xie Cheng's book says: "Jun styled Xiaoyuan, a man of Wushang.
translations.idiomatic: Xie Cheng describes him: "Luo Jun, courtesy name Xiaoyuan, from Wushang."

sentences.zh: 察孝廉，補尚書侍郎，擢拜陳國相。

translations.literal: Recommended filial and incorrupt, supplemented as Gentleman of the Secretariat, promoted and appointed Chen kingdom chancellor.
translations.idiomatic: Recommended as filial and incorrupt, he rose from secretariat clerk to chancellor of Chen.

sentences.zh: 人有產子，厚致米肉，達府主意，生男女者，以駱為名。

translations.literal: When people had childbirth, he thickly sent rice and meat, reaching the mansion master's intent; those who bore male or female took Luo as the name.
translations.idiomatic: He sent generous gifts of grain and meat to every newborn household so that parents named sons and daughters "Luo" in gratitude.

sentences.zh: 袁術使部曲將張闓陽私行到陳，之俊所，俊往從飲酒，因詐殺俊，一郡吏人哀號如喪父母。」

translations.literal: Yuan Shu ordered subordinate general Zhang Kai falsely to go in private to Chen, to Jun's place; Jun went to follow drinking wine, and Kai deceptively killed Jun; one commandery officials and people wailed as if mourning father and mother.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Shu sent his officer Zhang Kai to Chen on a covert mission; Luo Jun went to drink with him, and Kai treacherously slew him, whereupon the whole commandery wailed as if for parents.

sentences.zh: 是時諸國無復租祿，而數見虜奪，并日而食，轉死溝壑者甚觿。

translations.literal: At this time the various states no longer had rent stipends, and repeatedly saw plunder and seizure, eating every other day, those who turned and died in ditches and gullies were very many.
translations.idiomatic: Princes no longer received stipends from the court and were repeatedly plundered; many ate only every other day and perished in the ditches.

sentences.zh: 夫人姬妾多為丹*

translations.literal: Wives and concubines were often taken captive; many [text reads 丹 with lacuna].
translations.idiomatic: Women of the inner quarters were frequently abducted; the manuscript breaks off after the first syllable of a place-name.

translations.literal: (Yang)
translations.idiomatic: The next fragment reads the syllable yang in parentheses, evidently completing the place-name Danyang.

sentences.zh: **[陵]*兵烏桓所略雲。

translations.literal: **[Ling]* troops by the Wuhuan were plundered, it is said.
translations.idiomatic: The damaged text continues with troops (perhaps Danyang soldiers) reportedly carried off by Wuhuan raiders.

sentences.zh: 彭城靖王恭

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Pengcheng, Gong.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jing of Pengcheng, Liu Gong.

sentences.zh: 彭城靖王恭，永平九年賜號靈壽王。

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Pengcheng, Gong, in the ninth year of Yongping was granted the title Prince of Lingshou.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong, later Prince Jing of Pengcheng, received the honorary title Prince of Lingshou in 66 CE.

sentences.zh: [一]十五年，封為鉅鹿王。

translations.literal: [One] In the fifteenth year, he was enfeoffed as Prince of Julu.
translations.idiomatic: In 72 CE he was made Prince of Julu.

sentences.zh: 建初三年，徙封江陵王，改南郡為國。

translations.literal: In the third year of Jianchu, he was transferred enfeoffment as Prince of Jiangling, changing Nan commandery into a state.
translations.idiomatic: In 78 CE his title was shifted to Prince of Jiangling, with Nan commandery converted into his princedom.

sentences.zh: 元和二年，三公上言江陵在京師正南，不可以封，乃徙為六安王，以廬江郡為國。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yuanhe, the Three Excellencies spoke up that Jiangling was directly south of the capital and could not be enfeoffed, thereupon he was transferred to be Prince of Lu'an, using Lujiang commandery as the state.
translations.idiomatic: In 85 CE the Three Excellencies objected that a capital directly south of Luoyang could not host a prince, so he became Prince of Lu'an with Lujiang commandery as his fief.

sentences.zh: 肅宗崩，遺詔徙封彭城王，食楚郡，其年就國。

translations.literal: When Suzong died, a testamentary edict transferred his enfeoffment to Prince of Pengcheng, eating Chu commandery, that year going to the state.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Zhang's will moved him to Pengcheng with Chu commandery as his appanage, and he took up residence the same year.

sentences.zh: 恭敦厚威重，舉動有節度，吏人敬愛之。

translations.literal: Gong was generous and thick, awesome and weighty, movements had moderation, clerks and people respected and loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong was stolid, dignified, and measured in every gesture; officials and commoners alike held him in affection and awe.

sentences.zh: 永初六年，封恭子阿奴為竹邑侯。

translations.literal: In the sixth year of Yongchu, Gong's son Anu was enfeoffed as Marquis of Zhuyi.
translations.idiomatic: In 112 CE his son Liu Anu was enfeoffed as Marquis of Zhuyi.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]取其美名也，下重熹王亦同。

translations.literal: [Two] Comment one: They chose a fine-sounding title; the same applies to the Prince of Chongxi below.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The initial grant used an honorific place-name; the same pattern applies to the Prince of Chongxi (Liu Dang) treated later in this chapter.

sentences.zh: 東觀記曰「賜號，未有國邑」也。

translations.literal: The Eastern View record says: "Granted title, not yet having state territory" also.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Annals note that at first he held an honorific title but no territorial fief.

sentences.zh: 注[二]竹邑，縣，屬沛郡，故城在今徐州符離縣也。

translations.literal: Comment two: Zhuyi, a county, belonged to Pei commandery; the old walled town is today in Fuli County of Xuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Zhuyi County was in Pei commandery; its old city lay in what is now Fuli, Xuzhou.

sentences.zh: 「竹邑」或為「邕」字，轉寫誤也。

translations.literal: "Zhuyi" perhaps is the character "Yong", a transcription error.
translations.idiomatic: Some manuscripts miscopy the placename Zhuyi as a homophonous graph meaning harmonious consonance.

sentences.zh: 元初三年，恭以事怒子酺，酺自殺。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanchu, Gong because of affairs was angry at son Pu, Pu committed suicide.
translations.idiomatic: In 116 CE Liu Gong quarreled with his son Liu Pu over a domestic matter, and the young man killed himself.

sentences.zh: [一]國相趙牧以狀上，因誣奏恭祠祀惡言，大逆不道。

translations.literal: [One] The kingdom chancellor Zhao Mu reported the situation upward, thereby falsely memorializing Gong's temple sacrifice evil words, great rebellion lack of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Zhao Mu sent up a report, then falsely accused Liu Gong of uttering sacrilegious words at a shrine—capital treason.

sentences.zh: 有司奏請誅之。

translations.literal: The responsible officials memorialized requesting to execute him.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry recommended his execution.

sentences.zh: 恭上書自訟。

translations.literal: Gong submitted a memorial self-pleading.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Gong memorialized in his own defense.

sentences.zh: 朝廷以其素著行義，令考實，無征，牧坐下獄，會赦免死。

translations.literal: The court because he by nature was noted for conduct and righteousness, ordered examination of the facts, without verification, Mu sat and entered prison, meeting amnesty spared death.
translations.idiomatic: The court knew his reputation for integrity, ordered a thorough inquiry, found no evidence, and jailed Zhao Mu; an amnesty spared him execution.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]東觀記曰：「恭子男丁前*[妻]*物故，酺侮慢丁小妻，恭怒，閉酺馬廄，酺亡，夜詣彭城縣欲上書，恭遣從官倉頭曉令歸，數責之，乃自殺也。」

translations.literal: [Two] Comment one: The Eastern View record says: "Gong's son Ding's former wife had died; Pu insulted Ding's concubine; Gong was angry and confined Pu in the stable. Pu fled, went by night to Pengcheng County to submit a memorial; Gong sent a household steward at dawn to order him home, rebuked him repeatedly, and he then committed suicide."
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge Annals relate that Liu Pu had slighted his late brother Ding's concubine; the prince locked him in the stable. He fled at night to petition at the county seat, was escorted home at daybreak and sharply rebuked, and then took his own life.

sentences.zh: 注[二]決錄注曰：「牧字仲師，長安人。

translations.literal: Comment two: The Juelu commentary says: "Mu styled Zhongshi, a man of Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: The Juelu commentary identifies Zhao Mu, courtesy name Zhongshi, from Chang'an.

sentences.zh: 少知名，以公正稱。

translations.literal: In youth was known by name, by fairness and uprightness was called.
translations.idiomatic: He was known young for impartiality.

sentences.zh: 修春秋，事樂恢。

translations.literal: Studied the Spring and Autumn, served Le Hui.
translations.idiomatic: He studied the Spring and Autumn Annals under Le Hui.

sentences.zh: 恢以直諫死，牧為陳冤得申。

translations.literal: Hui because of straight remonstrance died; Mu for him stated grievance and obtained extension.
translations.idiomatic: When Le Hui died for his blunt remonstrance, Zhao Mu petitioned until his wrong was redressed.

sentences.zh: 高第為侍御史﹑會稽太守，皆有稱績。

translations.literal: In high grade became attendant censor, Kuaiji grand warden, all had praised achievements.
translations.idiomatic: He ranked at the top of the selection lists as palace censor and as governor of Kuaiji, earning a strong reputation in both posts.

sentences.zh: 及誣奏恭，安帝疑其侵，乃遣御史母丘歆覆案其事實，下牧廷尉，會赦不誅，終於家。」

translations.literal: When he falsely memorialized Gong, Andi suspected his encroachment, then sent Censor Muqiu Xin to re-investigate the affair's facts, cast Mu down to the Commandant of Justice, met amnesty not executed, ended at home.
translations.idiomatic: After he framed Liu Gong, Emperor An suspected overreach and sent the censor Muqiu Xin to reopen the case; Zhao Mu was remanded to the Commandant of Justice but escaped execution under an amnesty and died at home.

sentences.zh: 道立二十八年薨，子頃王定嗣。

translations.literal: Dao, having been established twenty-eight years, died; his son Prince Qing, Ding, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Dao reigned twenty-eight years and was succeeded by his son Liu Ding, posthumously Prince Qing.

sentences.zh: 本初元年，封定兄弟九人皆為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Benchu, Ding's nine brothers were all enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 146 CE nine of Liu Ding's brothers received village marquisates.

sentences.zh: [一] 注[一]東觀記曰「定兄據卞亭侯，弟光昭陽亭侯，固公梁亭侯，興蒲亭侯，延昌城亭侯，祀梁父亭侯，堅西安亭侯，代林亭侯」也。

translations.literal: [One] Comment one: The Eastern View record says: "Ding's elder brother Ju was Marquis of Bian village, younger brother Guang was Marquis of Zhaoyang village, Gu was Marquis of Gongliang village, Xing was Marquis of Pu village, Yan was Marquis of Changcheng village, Si was Marquis of Liangfu village, Jian was Marquis of Xi'an village, Dai was Marquis of Lin village" also.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The Eastern Lodge Annals enumerate Ding's brothers' village marquisates: Bian, Zhaoyang, Gongliang, Pu, Changcheng, Liangfu, Xi'an, and Lin.

sentences.zh: 定立四年薨，子孝王和嗣。

translations.literal: Ding, having been established four years, died; his son Prince Xiao, He, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ding died after four years and was succeeded by his son Liu He, posthumously Prince Xiao.

sentences.zh: 和性至孝，太夫人薨，行喪陵次，毀胔過禮。

translations.literal: He by nature was utmost filial; when the great lady mother died, he performed mourning at the tomb side, emaciation exceeding the rites.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He was deeply filial; when his mother died he kept vigil at her tumulus and wasted away beyond what ritual prescribed.

sentences.zh: 傅相以聞。

translations.literal: The tutor and chancellor reported it to be heard.
translations.idiomatic: His tutor and chancellor reported the matter to the throne.

sentences.zh: 桓帝詔使奉牛酒迎王還宮。

translations.literal: Huandi ordered envoys to present oxen and wine to welcome the king back to the palace.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Huan sent messengers with cattle and wine to escort the prince back to his residence.

sentences.zh: 和敬賢樂施，國中愛之。

translations.literal: He honored the worthy and delighted in generosity; the people of his kingdom loved him.
translations.idiomatic: Liu He respected men of talent and gave freely; his subjects cherished him for it.

sentences.zh: 初平中，天下大亂，和為賊昌務所攻，避奔東阿，後得還國。

translations.literal: In the Chuping era the realm was in great chaos; He was attacked by the bandit Chang Wu, fled to take refuge in Dong'e, and afterward was able to return to his state.
translations.idiomatic: During the turmoil of Chuping (190–193 CE) he was driven out by the warlord Chang Wu, fled to Dong'e, and eventually regained his seat at Pengcheng.

sentences.zh: 立六十四年薨，孫祗嗣。

translations.literal: Having been established sixty-four years, he died; his grandson Zhi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: After a reign of sixty-four years he died and was succeeded by his grandson Liu Zhi.

sentences.zh: 立七年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Having been established seven years, Wei received the abdication; he was made Marquis of Chongde.
translations.idiomatic: Seven years later the Han yielded to Wei, and Liu Zhi was reduced to Marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 樂成靖王黨

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Lecheng, Dang.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jing of Lecheng, Liu Dang.

sentences.zh: 樂成靖王黨，永平九年賜號重熹王，十五年封樂成王。

translations.literal: Prince Jing of Lecheng, Dang, in the ninth year of Yongping was granted the title Prince of Chongxi, in the fifteenth year was enfeoffed as Prince of Lecheng.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Dang received the honorific title Prince of Chongxi in 66 CE and became Prince of Lecheng in 72 CE.

sentences.zh: 黨聰惠，善史書，喜正文字。

translations.literal: Dang was clever and quick; he was skilled at clerical script and delighted in correcting written forms.
translations.idiomatic: He was bright and deft, excelled at the clerical hand, and took pleasure in emending characters.

sentences.zh: 與肅宗同年，尤相親愛。

translations.literal: He was the same birth year as Suzong and was especially intimate and loving.
translations.idiomatic: He was born the same year as Emperor Zhang, and the two brothers were exceptionally close.

sentences.zh: 建初四年，以清河之遊﹑觀津，勃海之東光﹑成平，涿郡之中水﹑饒陽﹑安平﹑南深澤八縣益樂成國。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Jianchu, with the eight counties You and Guanjin of Qinghe, Dongguang and Chengping of Bohai, and Zhongshui, Raoyang, Anping, and Nanshenze of Zhuo commandery, they augmented the state of Lecheng.
translations.idiomatic: In 79 CE eight counties from Qinghe, Bohai, and Zhuo were added to his appanage to enlarge the kingdom of Lecheng.

sentences.zh: [一]及帝崩，其年就國。

translations.literal: [One] When the emperor died, that year he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died later that same year, he left the capital for his fief.

sentences.zh: 黨急刻不遵法度。

translations.literal: Dang was harsh and pressing and did not observe the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Dang was severe and arbitrary and scanted the law.

sentences.zh: 舊禁宮人出嫁，不得適諸國。

translations.literal: By former prohibition palace women who married might not be taken into the various states.
translations.idiomatic: An old rule barred former palace women from entering princely households through marriage.

sentences.zh: 有故掖庭技人哀置，嫁為男子章初妻，[二]黨召哀置入宮與通，初欲上書告之，黨恐懼，乃密賂哀置姊焦使殺初。

translations.literal: There was a former harem entertainer Ai Zhi, who had married as wife of the commoner Zhang Chu; [two] Dang summoned Ai Zhi into the palace and had intercourse with her; Chu wished to submit a memorial reporting it; Dang was fearful and then secretly bribed Ai Zhi's elder sister Jiao to have Chu killed.
translations.idiomatic: A onetime court musician named Ai Zhi had married Zhang Chu; Liu Dang called her into the inner palace for an affair. When Chu threatened to petition, the prince panicked and paid Ai Zhi's sister Jiao to murder him.

sentences.zh: 事發覺，黨乃縊殺內侍三人，以絕口語。

translations.literal: When the affair was discovered, Dang then strangled three inner attendants to death, in order to cut off testimony.
translations.idiomatic: Once the plot surfaced, he strangled three palace servants to silence witnesses.

sentences.zh: 又取故中山簡王傅婢李羽生為小妻。

translations.literal: He further took Li Yusheng, a bondmaid from the former Prince of Zhongshan Jian's tutor household, as a minor wife.
translations.idiomatic: He also took Li Yusheng, a maidservant from the establishment of Prince Jian of Zhongshan's tutor, as a concubine.

sentences.zh: 永元七年，國相舉奏之。

translations.literal: In the seventh year of Yongyuan, the kingdom chancellor memorialized him.
translations.idiomatic: In 95 CE his chancellor impeached him to the throne.

sentences.zh: 和帝詔削東光﹑鄡二縣。

translations.literal: Emperor He ordered stripping away the two counties Dongguang and Qiao.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He punished him by confiscating Dongguang and Qiao counties.

sentences.zh: [三] 注[一]前書及郡國志清河無游縣。

translations.literal: [Three] Comment one: The Former Documents and the Treatise on Commanderies and Kingdoms have no You county in Qinghe.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Neither the Han shu geography nor the Hou Han treatise lists a You county in Qinghe.

sentences.zh: 觀津故城在今德州蓨縣東北，東光在滄州東光縣南，成平在景城縣南，中水在今瀛州樂壽縣西北，南深澤在今定州深澤縣東也。

translations.literal: The old city of Guanjin is northeast of Xiu County in Dezhou today; Dongguang is south of Dongguang County in Cangzhou; Chengping is south of Jingcheng County; Zhongshui is northwest of Leshou County in modern Yingzhou; Nanshenze is east of Shenze County in Dingzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Guanjin's old site lay northeast of present Xiu in Hebei; the other seats are located as the commentary indicates across Hebei and Shandong.

sentences.zh: 注[二]哀，姓；

translations.literal: Comment two: Ai is the surname;
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: Ai is the surname;

sentences.zh: 置，名也。

translations.literal: Zhi is the given name.
translations.idiomatic: Zhi was her personal name.

sentences.zh: 稱男子者，無官爵也。

translations.literal: Calling him "man" means he had no office or noble rank.
translations.idiomatic: The text calls Zhang Chu a commoner because he held no title or office.

sentences.zh: 注[三]鄡縣屬鉅鹿郡。

translations.literal: Comment three: Qiao County belonged to Julu commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Qiao lay within Julu commandery.

sentences.zh: 鄡音羌堯反。

translations.literal: Qiao is read with the qiang-yao fanqie gloss.
translations.idiomatic: The syllable Qiao is spelled out with the qiang-yao fanqie reading.

sentences.zh: 立二十五年薨，子哀王崇嗣。

translations.literal: Having been established twenty-five years, he died; his son Prince Ai, Chong, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Dang reigned twenty-five years and was succeeded by his son Liu Chong, posthumously Prince Ai.

sentences.zh: 立二月薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Having been established two months, he died; without a son, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: He died after two months without an heir, and the kingdom lapsed.

sentences.zh: 明年，和帝立崇兄修侯巡為樂成王，是為厘王。

translations.literal: The next year, Emperor He established Chong's elder brother Xun, Marquis of Xiu, as Prince of Lecheng; this is Prince Li.
translations.idiomatic: The following year Emperor He revived the line by making Liu Xun, Marquis of Xiu and Liu Chong's elder brother, Prince of Lecheng, later honored as Prince Li.

sentences.zh: [一]立十五年薨，子隱王賓嗣。

translations.literal: [One] Having been established fifteen years, he died; his son Prince Yin, Bin, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Xun reigned fifteen years and was succeeded by his son Liu Bin, posthumously Prince Yin.

sentences.zh: 立八年薨，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: Having been established eight years, he died; without a son, the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Eight years later he too died without an heir and the fief was again suspended.

sentences.zh: 注[一]修縣*

translations.literal: Comment one: Xiu County (text reads Xiu with lacuna marker)
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The commentary cites Xiu County where the manuscript is damaged.

translations.literal: (and)
translations.idiomatic: The lacuna continues with the conjunction and.

translations.literal: The gloss reads namely Tiao County, with lacunae in the manuscript.
translations.idiomatic: The gloss identifies the county as Tiao, with the manuscript damaged.

translations.literal: (both)
translations.idiomatic: The next fragment marks both as belonging together.

sentences.zh: *屬勃海。

translations.literal: The fragment concludes that they belonged to Bohai.
translations.idiomatic: Those seats all lay in Bohai commandery.

sentences.zh: 條字或作「修」。

translations.literal: The character for tiao is sometimes written xiu, a copyist's error.
translations.idiomatic: Some editions write the county name as Xiu instead of Tiao.

sentences.zh: 明年，復立濟北惠王子萇為樂成王后。

translations.literal: The next year, they again established Chang, son of Prince Hui of Jibei, as successor to the Lecheng line.
translations.idiomatic: A year later the court re-established the kingdom under Liu Chang, a son of Prince Hui of Jibei.

sentences.zh: 萇到國數月，驕淫不法，愆過累積，冀州刺史與國相舉奏萇罪至不道。

translations.literal: Several months after Chang arrived in his state, he was arrogant, dissolute, and lawless; faults piled up until the governor of Ji Province and the kingdom chancellor jointly memorialized that his crimes reached lack of the Way.
translations.idiomatic: Within months of his arrival Liu Chang flouted every rule; the provincial governor and his chancellor impeached him for conduct worthy of capital charges.

sentences.zh: 安帝詔曰：「萇有靦其面，而放逸其心。

translations.literal: Emperor An's edict said: "Chang wears a brazen face while his heart runs wild.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor An proclaimed: "Liu Chang is shameless in countenance and unrestrained in mind.

sentences.zh: [一]知陵廟至重，承繼有禮，不惟致敬之節，肅穆之慎，乃敢擅損犧牲，不備苾芬。

translations.literal: [One] He knows the imperial shrines are supremely weighty and that legitimate succession follows ritual, yet he shows neither the reverence due at sacrifice nor the solemn care required; he dares to cut back sacrificial victims and leave the offerings without their proper fragrance.
translations.idiomatic: He understands how grave the ancestral cult is and how orderly inheritance must be, yet he skimps on oxen and sheep and lets the incense of the offerings go wanting.

sentences.zh: [二] 慢易大姬，不震厥教。

translations.literal: [Two] He treats his nominal mother with contempt and does not stand in awe of her instruction.
translations.idiomatic: He insults his adoptive mother and ignores her discipline.

sentences.zh: [三]出入顛覆，風淫於家，娉取人妻，饋遺婢妾。

translations.literal: [Three] He comes and goes in disorder, lets lust blow through his household, seizes other men's wives, and showers gifts on concubines.
translations.idiomatic: He turns the palace upside down, debauches his inner quarters, abducts wives of others, and lavishes bribes on servant girls.

sentences.zh: 毆擊吏人，專己凶暴。

translations.literal: He strikes clerks and commoners and indulges his own cruelty and violence.
translations.idiomatic: He beats officials and subjects alike, ruling by caprice and terror.

sentences.zh: 愆罪莫大，甚可恥也。

translations.literal: No fault could be greater than his; it is deeply shameful.
translations.idiomatic: His offenses admit no excuse; the disgrace is extreme.

sentences.zh: 朕覽八辟之議，不忍致之於理。

translations.literal: I have reviewed the "eight grounds for deliberation" and cannot bear to hand him over for execution.
translations.idiomatic: After weighing the eight mitigating categories in the Zhou statutes, I shrink from sending him to the executioner.

sentences.zh: [四] 其貶萇爵為臨湖侯。

translations.literal: [Four] Let his rank be reduced to Marquis of Linhu.
translations.idiomatic: He is therefore demoted to Marquis of Linhu.

sentences.zh: [五]朕無『則哲』之明，致簡統失序，罔以尉承大姬，增懷永歎。

translations.literal: [Five] I lack the perspicacity praised in the Documents as "knowing men"; I have let the succession fall into confusion and cannot console the dowager lady; my heart is heavy with long sighs.
translations.idiomatic: I do not possess the sage's gift for judging character, and my mismanagement has unsettled the succession and grieved his nominal mother beyond measure.

sentences.zh: 」[六] 注[一]靦，姡也。

translations.literal: Closing the edict; comment six, comment one: Mian means brazen-faced.
translations.idiomatic: The edict ends here; the commentary glosses the word for shamelessness as mian.

sentences.zh: 言面姡然無媿。

translations.literal: It means his face was brazenly without shame.
translations.idiomatic: That is, he looked unabashed.

sentences.zh: 姡音胡八反。

translations.literal: The gloss graph is read with the hu-ba fanqie spelling.
translations.idiomatic: The commentary syllable is pronounced with the hu-ba fanqie gloss.

sentences.zh: 注[二]詩小雅曰：「苾苾芬芬，祀事孔明。」

translations.literal: Comment two: The "Minor Odes" say: "Richly fragrant, richly fragrant, the sacrifice is brilliantly ordered."
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Book of Odes praises offerings as "fragrant indeed" when the ritual is flawless.

sentences.zh: 注[三]大姬即萇所繼之母。

translations.literal: Comment three: The great lady is the mother whom Chang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: The "great lady" is Liu Chang's adoptive mother.

sentences.zh: 震，懼也。

translations.literal: Zhen here means fear.
translations.idiomatic: The verb means to stand in awe.

sentences.zh: 注[四]周禮司寇：「以八辟麗邦法：一曰議親之辟，二曰議故之辟，三曰議賢之辟，四曰議能之辟，五曰議功之辟，六曰議貴之辟，七曰議勤之辟，八曰議賓之辟。」

translations.literal: Comment four: The Zhou li Minister of Crime: "Apply the eight deliberations to attach to the state's laws: first deliberation for kin, second for old associates, third for the worthy, fourth for the capable, fifth for merit, sixth for high rank, seventh for diligence, eighth for honored guests."
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: The Rites of Zhou list eight grounds on which punishment may be moderated for kinsmen, old friends, worthies, and the like.

sentences.zh: 注[五]臨湖屬廬江郡。

translations.literal: Comment five: Linhu belonged to Lujiang commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: Linhu County lay in Lujiang commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[六]袁宏《紀》曰：「尚書侍郎冷宏議，以為自非聖人，不能無過，故王太子生，為立賢師傅以訓導之。

translations.literal: Comment six: Yuan Hong's Annals say: "Gentleman of the Secretariat Leng Hong argued that unless one is a sage, one cannot be without fault; therefore when the heir apparent is born, wise tutors are appointed to instruct him.
translations.idiomatic: Note 6: Yuan Hong records Leng Hong's plea that princes need moral tutors because even sages err.

sentences.zh: 是以目不見惡，耳不聞非，能保其社稷，高明令終。

translations.literal: Thus his eyes do not see evil, his ears do not hear wrong, and he can preserve his altars of soil and grain and end his days in honor.
translations.idiomatic: With wise guidance the heir hears and sees no vice, and the altars remain secure.

sentences.zh: 萇少長藩國，內無過庭之訓，外無師傅之道，血氣方剛，卒受榮爵，幾微生過，遂陷不義。

translations.literal: Chang grew up inside a princedom, without the admonitions of the courtyard within and without the way of tutors without; his blood and qi were just firm, suddenly he received glorious nobility; from slight beginnings faults arose, and he fell into unrighteousness.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang was raised in a palace without the discipline of tutors at court or in the fief; young, proud, and suddenly ennobled, small lapses soon became grave crimes.

sentences.zh: 臣聞周官議親，憃愚見赦。

translations.literal: Your subject has heard that in the Zhou offices deliberation for kin extends even to the foolish and they may be pardoned.
translations.idiomatic: The statutes provide that even dull-witted kinsmen may be spared through the "deliberation for kin."

sentences.zh: 萇不殺無辜，以譴呵為非，無赫赫大惡，可裁削奪損其租賦，令得改過自新，革心向道。

translations.literal: Chang did not kill the innocent; he took reproof as offense; there is no glaring great evil; one may reduce and strip him, diminish his rent and tax, order him to reform himself anew and turn his heart toward the Way.
translations.idiomatic: He murdered no innocents and his worst faults are youthful arrogance rather than towering villainy; stripping his rents would punish him yet leave room for reform.

sentences.zh: 」案黃香集，香與宏共奏，此香之辭也。

translations.literal: " Checking Huang Xiang's collected works, Xiang together with Hong jointly memorialized; these are Xiang's words.
translations.idiomatic: Huang Xiang's literary collection shows that Huang Xiang co-signed this memorial with Leng Hong and that the language is Huang Xiang's.

sentences.zh: 延光元年，以河閒孝王子得嗣靖王后。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yanguang, Prince Xiao of Hejian's son De was appointed to continue Prince Jing's line.
translations.idiomatic: In 122 CE Liu De, a son of Prince Xiao of Hejian, was transferred to continue the cut-off line of Prince Jing of Lecheng.

sentences.zh: 以樂成比廢絕，故改國曰安平，是為安平孝王。

translations.literal: Because Lecheng had repeatedly been abolished, they therefore changed the state name to Anping; this is Prince Xiao of Anping.
translations.idiomatic: The kingdom was renamed Anping to mark a fresh start after Lecheng's repeated extinction; Liu De is honored as Prince Xiao of Anping.

sentences.zh: 立三十年薨，子續立。

translations.literal: Having been established thirty years, he died; his son Xu succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He reigned thirty years and was succeeded by his son Liu Xu.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，黃巾賊起，為所劫質，囚於廣宗。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping, when the Yellow Turban bandits arose, he was taken hostage by them and imprisoned in Guangzong.
translations.idiomatic: When the rebellion erupted in 184 CE, the prince was seized as a hostage and held at Guangzong.

sentences.zh: [一]賊平復國。

translations.literal: [One] When the bandits were pacified, he was restored to his state.
translations.idiomatic: After the rebels were crushed he returned to his throne.

sentences.zh: 其年秋，坐不道被誅。

translations.literal: That autumn, for conduct lacking the Way he was executed.
translations.idiomatic: The same autumn he was put to death for capital crimes.

sentences.zh: 立三十四年，國除。

translations.literal: Having been established thirty-four years, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: Thirty-four years of rule ended with the abolition of his fief.

sentences.zh: 注[一]今貝州宗城縣也，隨室諱改焉。

translations.literal: Comment one: It is today Zongcheng County in Beizhou, renamed under the Sui taboo.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Guangzong corresponds to present-day Zongcheng in southern Hebei, renamed under the Sui to avoid an imperial taboo.

sentences.zh: 下邳惠王衍

translations.literal: Prince Hui of Xiapi, Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Hui of Xiapi, Liu Yan.

sentences.zh: 下邳惠王衍，永平十五年封。

translations.literal: Prince Hui of Xiapi, Yan, in the fifteenth year of Yongping was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yan received Xiapi in the fifteenth year of Yongping (72 CE).

sentences.zh: 衍有容貌，肅宗即位，常在左右。

translations.literal: Yan had handsome features; when Suzong succeeded to the throne, he was constantly at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Handsome and winning, he was seldom away from Emperor Zhang after the latter ascended the throne.

sentences.zh: 建初初冠，詔賜衍師傅已下官屬金帛各有差。

translations.literal: At the beginning of Jianchu when he capped, an edict granted Yan and his tutor downward through his officials gold and silk each according to grade.
translations.idiomatic: At his capping in the early Jianchu era the court showered gold and silk on his tutors and household staff in graded gifts.

sentences.zh: 四年，以臨淮郡及九江之鐘離﹑當塗﹑東城﹑歷陽﹑全椒合十七縣益下邳國。

translations.literal: In the fourth year, with Linhuai commandery and from Jiujiang Zhongli, Dangtu, Dongcheng, Liyang, and Quanjiao, together seventeen counties, they augmented the state of Xiapi.
translations.idiomatic: In 79 CE seventeen counties from Linhuai and Jiujiang were added to enlarge the kingdom of Xiapi.

sentences.zh: [一]帝崩，其年就國。

translations.literal: [One] When the emperor died, that year he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died he left for his fief the same year.

sentences.zh: 衍後病荒忽，而太子卬有罪廢，諸姬爭欲立子為嗣，連上書相告言。

translations.literal: Later Yan fell ill with confusion and wandering mind, while the heir apparent Ang was guilty and deposed; the various concubines vied wishing to establish their sons as heir, repeatedly submitting memorials accusing one another.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yan later lost his wits, and after Crown Prince Liu Ang was deposed for crime his consorts fought to install their own sons, bombarding the court with mutual accusations.

sentences.zh: 和帝憐之，使彭城靖王恭至下邳正其嫡庶，立子成為太子。

translations.literal: Emperor He pitied him and sent Prince Jing of Pengcheng, Gong, to Xiapi to rectify legitimate versus concubine-born heirs, establishing the son Cheng as heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He took pity and dispatched Liu Gong of Pengcheng to settle the succession, naming Liu Cheng crown prince.

sentences.zh: [二] 注[一]鐘離在今豪州鐘離縣東。

translations.literal: [Two] Comment one: Zhongli is east of Zhongli County in modern Haozhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Ancient Zhongli lay east of present Zhongli in Anhui (commentary writes Hao for Chu).

sentences.zh: 當塗在縣西南。

translations.literal: Dangtu is southwest of the county seat.
translations.idiomatic: Dangtu stood southwest of the county town.

sentences.zh: 東城在定遠縣東南。

translations.literal: Dongcheng is southeast of Dingyuan County.
translations.idiomatic: Dongcheng lay southeast of present Dingyuan.

sentences.zh: 歷陽，和州縣也。

translations.literal: Liyang is a county of Hezhou.
translations.idiomatic: Liyang is the seat in modern Chaohu (Hezhou).

sentences.zh: 全椒，今滁州縣也。

translations.literal: Quanjiao is a county of Chuzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Quanjiao is today's Quanjiao county under Chuzhou.

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀記載賜恭詔曰：「皇帝問彭城王始夏無恙。

translations.literal: Comment two: The Eastern View record preserves the edict granted to Gong: "The emperor asks whether the Prince of Pengcheng is well after the beginning of summer.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The Eastern Lodge Annals quote Emperor He's letter to Liu Gong opening with seasonal greetings.

sentences.zh: 蓋聞堯親九族，萬國協和，書典之所美也。

translations.literal: We have heard that Yao drew his nine kin close and the myriad states harmonized; this is what the canonical documents praise.
translations.idiomatic: The classics praise Yao for harmonizing the realm by cherishing his kin.

sentences.zh: 下邳王被病沉滯之疾，昏亂不明，家用不寧，姬妾適庶，諸子分爭，紛紛至今。

translations.literal: The Prince of Xiapi is afflicted with a lingering illness of confusion and dimness; his household is not tranquil; concubines, legitimate and secondary, and the various sons divide and contend, in turmoil down to the present.
translations.idiomatic: The Prince of Xiapi is senile; his harem is in chaos as principal wives and concubines promote rival sons.

sentences.zh: 前太子卬頑凶失道，陷於大辟，是後諸子更相誣告，迄今適嗣未知所定，朕甚傷之。

translations.literal: The former heir apparent Ang was stubborn and vicious, lost the Way, and fell into great execution; afterward the various sons have alternately slandered one another; until now the rightful successor is not yet settled; We are deeply grieved.
translations.idiomatic: Crown Prince Ang proved vicious and paid with his life; since then every princeling has slandered the rest, and no lawful heir has been settled, which grieves Us deeply.

sentences.zh: 惟王與下邳王恩義至親，正此國嗣，非王而誰？

translations.literal: Only the king and the Prince of Xiapi share the utmost grace and duty of kin; to rectify this state's succession, who if not the king?
translations.idiomatic: You and the Prince of Xiapi are closest in blood; who but you can settle his house?

sentences.zh: 禮重適庶之序，春秋之義大居正。

translations.literal: The rites stress the order of legitimate versus concubine-born heirs; the Spring and Autumn Annals take greatest residence in the upright.
translations.idiomatic: Ritual privileges the true-born heir, and the Spring and Autumn tradition exalts the legitimate line.

sentences.zh: 孔子曰：『惟仁者能好人，能惡人。

translations.literal: Confucius said: "Only the humane can truly love people and can truly despise people."
translations.idiomatic: Confucius said that only the humane know whom to love and whom to reject.

sentences.zh: 』貴仁者所好惡得其中也。

translations.literal: " For the noble man, what he loves and hates attains the mean.
translations.idiomatic: For a gentleman, love and hatred stay within the bounds of justice.

sentences.zh: 太子國之儲嗣，可不慎歟！

translations.literal: The heir apparent is the state's reserve successor; can one not be cautious?
translations.idiomatic: The crown prince is the bulwark of the kingdom; the choice admits no negligence.

sentences.zh: 王其差次下邳諸子可為太子者上名，將及景風拜授印綬焉。」

translations.literal: Let the king rank in order the sons of Xiapi who may serve as heir apparent and submit their names upward; We shall invest them with seals and ribbons at the Jingfeng season."
translations.idiomatic: Rank the eligible sons of Xiapi and memorialize their names; We shall bestow seals and ribbons at the midsummer Jingfeng audience.

sentences.zh: 衍立五十四年薨，子貞王成嗣。

translations.literal: Yan, having been established fifty-four years, died; his son Prince Zhen, Cheng, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yan reigned fifty-four years and was succeeded by Liu Cheng, posthumously Prince Zhen.

sentences.zh: 永建元年，封成兄二人及惠王孫二人皆為列侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongjian, Cheng's two elder brothers and Prince Hui's two grandsons were all enfeoffed as full marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 126 CE two of Liu Cheng's elder brothers and two grandsons of Prince Hui were each made full marquises.

sentences.zh: 成立二年薨，子愍王意嗣。

translations.literal: Cheng, having been established two years, died; his son Prince Min, Yi, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Cheng died after two years and was succeeded by Liu Yi, posthumously Prince Min.

sentences.zh: 陽嘉元年，封意弟八人為鄉﹑亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yangjia, eight younger brothers of Yi were enfeoffed as village and pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 132 CE eight of Liu Yi's younger brothers received village or pavilion marquisates.

sentences.zh: 中平元年，意遭黃巾，□國走。

translations.literal: In the first year of Zhongping, Yi encountered the Yellow Turbans and abandoned his state and fled (one character missing in the received text).
translations.idiomatic: In 184 CE Liu Yi fled his capital before the Yellow Turbans (the verb is damaged in the manuscript).

sentences.zh: 賊平復國，數月薨。

translations.literal: When the bandits were pacified he was restored to his state; several months later he died.
translations.idiomatic: He returned after the rebellion but died within a few months.

sentences.zh: 立五十七年，年九十。

translations.literal: Having been established fifty-seven years, age ninety.
translations.idiomatic: He had reigned fifty-seven years and died at ninety.

sentences.zh: 子哀王宜嗣，數月薨，無子，建安十一年國除。

translations.literal: His son Prince Ai, Yi, succeeded; several months later he died; without a son, in the eleventh year of Jian'an the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: His son Liu Yi, the Prince Ai whose name is written with the graph yi meaning fitting, survived him only months without an heir, and the line ended in 206 CE.

sentences.zh: 梁節王暢

translations.literal: Prince Jie of Liang, Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Jie of Liang, Liu Chang.

sentences.zh: 梁節王暢，永平十五年封為汝南王。

translations.literal: Prince Jie of Liang, Chang, in the fifteenth year of Yongping was enfeoffed as Prince of Runan.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang was first made Prince of Runan in 72 CE.

sentences.zh: 母陰貴人有寵，暢尤被愛幸，國土租入倍於諸國。

translations.literal: His mother, the Honored Lady Yin, had favor; Chang especially received love and fortune, his state land rent income doubled that of the other states.
translations.idiomatic: Because his mother Lady Yin was a favorite of the emperor, Liu Chang was doubly cherished and his kingdom drew twice the revenue of other princes.

sentences.zh: 肅宗立，緣先帝之意，賞賜恩寵甚篤。

translations.literal: When Suzong was established, following the late emperor's intent, rewards and grants of grace and favor were very generous.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Zhang came to the throne he heaped gifts on Liu Chang in keeping with his father's wishes.

sentences.zh: 建初二年，封暢舅陰棠為西陵侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianchu, Chang's maternal uncle Yin Tang was enfeoffed as Marquis of Xiling.
translations.idiomatic: In 77 CE his uncle Yin Tang received the marquisate of Xiling.

sentences.zh: [一]四年，徙為梁王，以陳留之郾﹑寧陵，濟陰之薄﹑單父﹑己氏﹑成武，凡六縣，益梁國。

translations.literal: [One] In the fourth year, he was transferred to be Prince of Liang, with Yan and Ningling of Chenliu, Bo, Shanfu, Jishi, and Chengwu of Jiyin, six counties in all, augmenting the state of Liang.
translations.idiomatic: In 79 CE he became Prince of Liang, gaining six counties from Chenliu and Jiyin to swell his appanage.

sentences.zh: [二]帝崩，其年就國。

translations.literal: [Two] When the emperor died, that year he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: He left for his fief the year the emperor died.

sentences.zh: 注[一]西陵，縣，屬江夏郡。

translations.literal: Comment one: Xiling, a county, belonged to Jiangxia commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: Xiling County lay in Jiangxia commandery.

sentences.zh: 注[二]□，今許州郾陵縣也。

translations.literal: Comment two: Yan (one character missing in received text), today Yanling County in Xuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The county glossed here is modern Yanling in Henan (one graph missing in the manuscript).

sentences.zh: 寧陵，今宋州縣也。

translations.literal: Ningling is a county of Songzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Ningling is now under Shangqiu (Songzhou).

sentences.zh: 薄故城在今曹州考城縣東北。

translations.literal: The old city of Bo is northeast of Kaocheng County in Caozhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Ancient Bo lay northeast of present Kaocheng in Shandong.

sentences.zh: 單父，今宋州縣也。

translations.literal: Shanfu is a county of Songzhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Shanfu is now a county under Shangqiu.

sentences.zh: 己氏，今宋州楚丘縣也。

translations.literal: Jishi is today Chuqiu County in Songzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Jishi corresponds to Chuqiu in Henan.

sentences.zh: 成武，今曹州縣也。

translations.literal: Chengwu is a county of Caozhou today.
translations.idiomatic: Chengwu is now under Heze (Caozhou).

sentences.zh: 暢性聰惠，然少貴驕，頗不遵法度。

translations.literal: Chang by nature was clever and quick, yet from youth noble and arrogant, rather did not observe the statutes.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang was bright but spoiled, highborn and arrogant, and often flouted the law.

sentences.zh: 歸國後，數有惡夢，從官卞忌自言能使六丁，善占夢，[一]暢數使卜筮。

translations.literal: After returning to his state, repeatedly he had evil dreams; follower official Bian Ji declared himself able to command the six Ding spirits and skilled at interpreting dreams; [one] Chang repeatedly had him perform divination.
translations.idiomatic: Once in his kingdom he suffered nightmares; his retainer Bian Ji claimed mastery of the six Ding gods and dream divination, and the prince consulted him again and again.

sentences.zh: 又暢乳母王禮等，因此自言能見鬼神事，遂共佔氣，祠祭求福。

translations.literal: Further Chang's wet-nurse Wang Li and others, on this account declared themselves able to see ghost and spirit affairs, then together divined auspiciousness, sacrificed and prayed for blessing.
translations.idiomatic: His nurse Wang Li and her circle claimed they could see spirits; together they read omens and offered illicit sacrifices for good fortune.

sentences.zh: 忌等諂媚，雲神言王當為天子。

translations.literal: Ji and the others flattered, saying the spirits declared the king would become Son of Heaven.
translations.idiomatic: They flattered him with forged spirit messages that he was destined for the throne.

sentences.zh: 暢心喜，與相應荅。

translations.literal: Chang's heart rejoiced and he answered them in accord.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang welcomed the lie and played along.

sentences.zh: 永元五年，豫州刺史梁相舉奏暢不道，考訊，辭不服。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Yongyuan, the governor of Yu Province and the Liang kingdom chancellor jointly memorialized that Chang lacked the Way; under examination and interrogation he would not confess.
translations.idiomatic: In 93 CE provincial and kingdom officials impeached him for capital crimes; under torture he still protested innocence.

sentences.zh: 有司請征暢詣廷尉詔獄，和帝不許。

translations.literal: The responsible officials requested summoning Chang to the Commandant of Justice imperial prison; Emperor He did not permit.
translations.idiomatic: The ministry asked to haul him to the capital prison, but Emperor He refused.

sentences.zh: 有司重奏除暢國，徙九真，帝不忍，但削成武﹑單父二縣。

translations.literal: The responsible officials again memorialized to remove Chang's state and exile him to Jiuzhen; the emperor could not bear it, but only stripped away the two counties Chengwu and Shanfu.
translations.idiomatic: They renewed their plea to abolish his kingdom and banish him to the far south; the emperor relented and punished him only by taking Chengwu and Shanfu.

sentences.zh: 暢籩懼，上疏辭謝曰：「臣天性狂愚，生在深宮，長養傅母之手，信惑左右之言。

translations.literal: Chang trembled with fear and submitted a memorial declining fault, saying: "Your subject by nature is mad and foolish, born in the deep palace, reared in wet-nurses' hands, believing and being deluded by words of those at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Terror-stricken, Liu Chang memorialized: "I was born foolish, raised inside the palace by nurses, and I believed the gossip of those around me.

sentences.zh: 及至歸國，不知防禁。

translations.literal: When it came to returning to my state, I did not know how to guard against prohibitions.
translations.idiomatic: Once I reached my fief I did not understand what was forbidden.

sentences.zh: 從官侍史利臣財物，熒惑臣暢。

translations.literal: Follower officials and attendant scribes coveted profit from your subject's goods and deluded your subject Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Retainers and clerks who coveted my wealth fed me lies.

sentences.zh: 臣暢無所昭見，與相然諾，不自知陷死罪，以至考案。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang had no clear insight, assented to them in agreement, did not himself know he had fallen into a capital crime, until the examination case.
translations.idiomatic: I lacked judgment, nodded along, and did not realize I had committed a capital offense until the inquiry began.

sentences.zh: 肌栗心悸，自悔無所復及。

translations.literal: Flesh shuddered and heart palpitated; self-repentance had nothing further to reach.
translations.idiomatic: I trembled with regret too late to undo the harm.

sentences.zh: 自謂當實時伏顯誅，魂魄去身，分歸黃泉。

translations.literal: I thought I would in due time submit to manifest execution, soul and spirit leave the body, each part returning to the yellow springs.
translations.idiomatic: I expected to die at once and join the shades below.

sentences.zh: 不意陛下聖德，枉法曲平，不聽有司，[二]橫貸赦臣。

translations.literal: I did not expect Your Majesty's sagely virtue, to bend law and extend grace, not listening to the responsible officials, [two] rashly pardoning your subject.
translations.idiomatic: I never dreamed Your Majesty would bend the statute, overrule the ministry, and spare me.

sentences.zh: 戰慄連月，未敢自安。

translations.literal: I tremble month on month and dare not feel secure.
translations.idiomatic: For months I have shaken with fear and cannot rest.

sentences.zh: 上念以負先帝而令陛下為臣收污天下，[三]誠無氣以息，筋骨不相連。

translations.literal: Above I think I have failed the late emperor and caused Your Majesty to gather vileness for your subject throughout the realm, [three] truly I have no breath to rest, sinews and bones do not connect.
translations.idiomatic: I have shamed my father's memory and blackened Your Majesty's name; I scarcely breathe and feel my body falling apart.

sentences.zh: 臣暢知大貸不可再得，自誓束身約妻子，不敢復出入失繩墨，不敢復有所橫費。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang knows a great pardon cannot be obtained twice; he swears to bind himself and restrain wife and children, dares not again transgress the inked line in going out and coming in, dares not again have any rash expenditure.
translations.idiomatic: I know I cannot count on mercy twice: I vow to discipline myself and my household, spend nothing unwisely, and never again step outside the law.

sentences.zh: 租入有餘，乞裁食睢陽﹑谷孰﹑虞﹑蒙﹑寧陵五縣，還余所食四縣。

translations.literal: Rent income is more than enough; I beg to cut away and eat only Suiyang, Gushi, Yu, Meng, and Ningling five counties, returning the surplus of the four counties I eat.
translations.idiomatic: My revenues still exceed my needs; I ask leave to yield five counties and keep only four for my maintenance.

sentences.zh: 臣暢小妻三十七人，其無子者願還本家。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang's minor wives number thirty-seven; those without sons I wish returned to their original families.
translations.idiomatic: Of my thirty-seven concubines, those who bore no sons may go home to their kin.

sentences.zh: 自選擇謹□奴婢二百人，其餘所受虎賁﹑官騎及諸工技﹑鼓吹﹑倉頭﹑奴婢﹑兵弩﹑廄馬皆上還本署。

translations.literal: I myself select two hundred careful bondservants (one character missing in received text); the remainder of tiger guards, official horsemen, various craftsmen, musicians, granary heads, slave-girls, crossbowmen, and stable horses I all return upward to their original offices.
translations.idiomatic: I will keep two hundred trustworthy domestics; every guard, groom, artisan, musician, and horse I received from the court I send back to the agencies that issued them.

sentences.zh: 臣暢以骨肉近親，亂聖化，污清流，既得生活，誠無心面目以凶惡復居大宮，食大國，張官屬，藏什物。

translations.literal: Your subject Chang, as flesh-and-bone close kin, has disordered sage transformation and stained the clear current; having obtained life, truly he has no heart or face to dwell again in a great palace, eat a great state, display official retinue, or hoard goods.
translations.idiomatic: As your kinsman I have corrupted public morals; having been spared I cannot bear to keep a princely household, vast revenues, or a full retinue.

sentences.zh: 願陛下加大恩，開臣自悔之門，假臣小善之路，令天下知臣蒙恩，得去死就生，頗能自悔。

translations.literal: I beg Your Majesty to increase great grace, open a gate for your subject's self-repentance, lend a path for your subject's small good, order the realm to know your subject received grace, was able to leave death and approach life, and can somewhat reform himself.
translations.idiomatic: Grant me a way to show repentance so the empire may see that Your Majesty raised me from death to life and that I mean to mend my ways.

sentences.zh: 臣以公卿所奏臣罪惡詔書常置於前，晝夜誦讀。

translations.literal: Your subject keeps the edict listing the crimes the excellencies reported against your subject always before him, reading it day and night.
translations.idiomatic: I keep the indictment the ministers filed and Your edict on my desk and read them night and day.

sentences.zh: 臣小人，貪見明時，不能實時自引，惟陛下哀臣，令得喘息漏刻。

translations.literal: Your subject is a petty man, greedy to see a bright age, unable in due time to withdraw himself; only may Your Majesty pity your subject and grant him a moment to catch his breath.
translations.idiomatic: I am too base to withdraw of my own accord; I beg only a little time to breathe before I answer for myself.

sentences.zh: 若不聽許，臣實無顏以久生，下入黃泉，無以見先帝。

translations.literal: If I am not permitted, your subject truly has no face to live long, descending to the yellow springs, having no means to see the late emperor.
translations.idiomatic: If you refuse, I cannot live with the shame or face my father in the grave.

sentences.zh: 此誠臣至心。

translations.literal: This is truly your subject's utmost heart.
translations.idiomatic: This is the utmost sincerity of my heart.

sentences.zh: 臣欲多還所受，恐天恩不聽許，節量所留，於臣暢饒足。

translations.literal: Your subject wishes to return much of what he received, fearing heavenly grace will not listen and permit; what is measured and retained is more than enough for your subject Chang.
translations.idiomatic: I would surrender more, yet fear you will not allow it; what I propose to keep still leaves me ample means.

sentences.zh: 」詔報曰：「朕惟王至親之屬，淳淑之美，傅相不良，不能防邪，至令有司紛紛有言。

translations.literal: The edict replied: "We consider the king among the utmost close kin, of pure and good beauty; tutor and chancellor were not good, unable to ward off evil, until it made the responsible officials speak repeatedly.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor answered: "You are closest in blood and of fine native character; bad tutors failed to shield you and brought the ministers down on you.

sentences.zh: 今王深思悔過，端自克責，朕惻然傷之。

translations.literal: Now the king deeply ponders repentance, corrects himself with self-blame; We are moved and grieved.
translations.idiomatic: Now that you repent and blame yourself, We are deeply moved.

sentences.zh: 志匪由*

translations.literal: The intent was not truly from the king (text damaged at this lacuna).
translations.idiomatic: The fault was not born in your own heart; the manuscript is broken here.

translations.literal: (from)
translations.idiomatic: The lacuna continues with the preposition from.

sentences.zh: **[王]*，咎在彼小子。

translations.literal: The blame lies with those petty fellows.
translations.idiomatic: The fault rests on your worthless attendants.

sentences.zh: [四]一日克己復禮，天下歸仁。

translations.literal: [Four] In one day restraining the self and returning to ritual, the realm turns to benevolence.
translations.idiomatic: The Analects say that mastering oneself restores propriety and wins the world's goodwill.

sentences.zh: 王其安心靜意，茂率休德。

translations.literal: Let the king quiet his heart and calm his intent, luxuriantly lead in fine virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Compose your mind, cultivate your virtue, and lead your people well.

sentences.zh: 易不雲乎：『一謙而四益。

translations.literal: Does not the Changes say: "One act of modesty brings fourfold gain.
translations.idiomatic: The Book of Changes praises modesty: one humble turn brings four kinds of blessing.

sentences.zh: 小有言，終吉。

translations.literal: There is slight criticism, yet in the end auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: Minor blame may be spoken, yet the end is good.

sentences.zh: 』[五]強食自愛。

translations.literal: "[Five] Eat well and cherish yourself.
translations.idiomatic: Take nourishment and care for yourself."

sentences.zh: 」暢固讓，章數上，卒不許。

translations.literal: Chang firmly declined; memorials went up several times; in the end he was not permitted.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang pressed his renunciation again and again but the court would not allow it.

sentences.zh: 注[一]六丁謂六甲中丁神也。

translations.literal: Comment one: The six Ding means the Ding spirits within the six jia cycle.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The six Ding are tutelary spirits of the sexagenary cycle.

sentences.zh: 若甲子旬中，則丁卯為神，甲寅旬中，則丁巳為神之類也。

translations.literal: For example in the jiazi decade then dingmao is the spirit; in the jiayin decade then dingsi is the spirit, and so on.
translations.idiomatic: Each ten-day stem set has its corresponding Ding spirit, as the commentary lists.

sentences.zh: 役使之法，先齋戒，然後其神至，可使致遠方物及知吉凶也。

translations.literal: The method of commanding them: first fast and purify, then the spirit arrives, and one can cause distant things to be brought and know good and ill.
translations.idiomatic: After fasting the adept can summon the spirit to fetch distant objects and foretell fortune.

sentences.zh: 注[二]曲平，曲法申恩，平處其罪。

translations.literal: Comment two: Qu ping means bending the law to extend grace and evenly settling his guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Note 2: The phrase means the emperor bent the rules to grant mercy while still recording the offense.

sentences.zh: 注[三]污，惡也。

translations.literal: Comment three: Wu means vileness.
translations.idiomatic: Note 3: Here vileness means moral stain.

sentences.zh: 天下以帝赦王為惡，故言收惡天下也。

translations.literal: The realm thought the emperor's pardoning the king was wrong, therefore it says gathering vileness throughout the realm.
translations.idiomatic: Critics said pardoning you tarnished imperial prestige everywhere.

sentences.zh: 注[四]謂由卞忌及王禮等也。

translations.literal: Comment four: It refers to Bian Ji and Wang Li and the others.
translations.idiomatic: Note 4: Meaning Bian Ji, Wang Li, and their confederates.

sentences.zh: 注[五]易謙卦曰：「天道虧盈而益謙，地道變盈而流謙，鬼神害盈而福謙，人道惡盈而好謙。

translations.literal: Comment five: The Changes modesty hexagram says: "Heaven's way lessens what is full and benefits the modest; earth's way changes what is full and flows toward the modest; spirits and ghosts harm what is full and bless the modest; human way hates what is full and loves the modest.
translations.idiomatic: Note 5: The commentary quotes the Book of Changes on how heaven, earth, spirits, and mankind all favor humility.

sentences.zh: 」為謙是一，而天地神人皆益之，故曰「一謙而四益」。

translations.literal: " For modesty it is one, yet heaven, earth, spirits, and humans all benefit it; therefore it is called "one modesty and four gains."
translations.idiomatic: Thus one humble stance wins favor from four realms of being.

sentences.zh: 訟卦初六曰：「小有言，終吉。

translations.literal: The Song hexagram, first six, says: "There is slight criticism, yet in the end auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: The Song line repeats that small blame ends well.

sentences.zh: 」言王雖小有訟言，而終吉也。

translations.literal: " It means that although the king had slight litigation talk, in the end it was auspicious.
translations.idiomatic: It applies to you: though fault was charged, the outcome was fortunate.

sentences.zh: 立二十七年薨，子恭王堅嗣。

translations.literal: Having been established twenty-seven years, he died; his son Prince Gong, Jian, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang reigned twenty-seven years and was succeeded by Liu Jian, posthumously Prince Gong.

sentences.zh: 永元十六年，封堅弟二人為鄉﹑亭侯。

translations.literal: In the sixteenth year of Yongyuan, two younger brothers of Jian were enfeoffed as village and pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 104 CE two of Liu Jian's brothers received village or pavilion marquisates.

sentences.zh: 堅立二十六年薨，子懷王匡嗣。

translations.literal: Jian, having been established twenty-six years, died; his son Prince Huai, Kuang, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Jian reigned twenty-six years and was succeeded by Liu Kuang, posthumously Prince Huai.

sentences.zh: 永建二年，封匡兄弟七人為鄉﹑亭侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongjian, seven brothers of Kuang were enfeoffed as village and pavilion marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 127 CE seven of Liu Kuang's brothers received village or pavilion marquisates.

sentences.zh: 匡立十一年薨，無子，順帝封匡弟孝陽亭侯成為梁王，是為夷王。

translations.literal: Kuang, having been established eleven years, died; without a son, Shundi enfeoffed Kuang's younger brother Cheng, Marquis of Xiaoyang village, as Prince of Liang; this is Prince Yi.
translations.idiomatic: After eleven years Liu Kuang died childless; Emperor Shun promoted his brother Liu Cheng from village marquis to Prince of Liang, later honored as Prince Yi.

sentences.zh: 立二十九年薨，子敬王元嗣。

translations.literal: Having been established twenty-nine years, he died; his son Prince Jing, Yuan, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Cheng reigned twenty-nine years and was succeeded by Liu Yuan, posthumously Prince Jing.

sentences.zh: 立十六年薨，子彌嗣。

translations.literal: Having been established sixteen years, he died; his son Mi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yuan died after sixteen years and was succeeded by Liu Mi.

sentences.zh: 立四十年，魏受禪，以為崇德侯。

translations.literal: Having been established forty years, Wei received the abdication; he was made Marquis of Chongde.
translations.idiomatic: Forty years later Wei ended the Han and Liu Mi became Marquis of Chongde.

sentences.zh: 淮陽頃王昞

translations.literal: Prince Qing of Huaiyang, Bing.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Qing of Huaiyang, Liu Bing.

sentences.zh: 淮陽頃王昞，永平*[十]*五年封常山王，建初四年，徙為淮陽王，以汝南之新安﹑西華益淮陽國。

translations.literal: Prince Qing of Huaiyang, Bing, in the fifteenth year of Yongping was enfeoffed as Prince of Changshan; in the fourth year of Jianchu he was transferred to be Prince of Huaiyang, with Xin'an and Xihua of Runan augmenting the state of Huaiyang.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bing was Prince of Changshan from 72 CE, moved to Huaiyang in 79 CE with two Runan counties added to his fief.

sentences.zh: 立十六年薨，未及立嗣，永元二年，和帝立□小子側復為常山王，奉□後，是為殤王。

translations.literal: Having been established sixteen years, he died; before an heir was installed, in the second year of Yongyuan Emperor He established Ce, young son of Bing, again as Prince of Changshan to continue Bing's line; this is Prince Shang.
