translations.idiomatic: After sixteen years on the throne he died before an heir could be named; in the second year of Yongyuan Emperor He installed his young son Liu Ce as Prince of Changshan to continue Liu Bing's line, posthumously Prince Shang.

sentences.zh: 立十三年薨，父子皆未之國，並葬京師。

translations.literal: Having been established thirteen years, he died; father and son both had not gone to their states, and were both buried at the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ce reigned thirteen years; neither father nor son ever took up their fiefs, and both were buried at Luoyang.

sentences.zh: 側無子，其月立兄防子侯章為常山王。

translations.literal: Ce had no son; that month his elder brother Fang's son, Marquis Zhang, was established as Prince of Changshan.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ce left no heir, so the same month Liu Zhang, son of Liu Fang and a marquis, was made Prince of Changshan.

sentences.zh: 和帝憐章早孤，數加賞賜。

translations.literal: Emperor He pitied Zhang for early orphanhood and repeatedly added rewards and grants.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor He pitied the boy's early loss of parents and showered him with gifts.

sentences.zh: 延平元年就國。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yanping he went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: He took up his seat at Changshan in 106 CE.

sentences.zh: 立二十五年薨，是為靖王。

translations.literal: Having been established twenty-five years, he died; this is Prince Jing.
translations.idiomatic: After twenty-five years he died and was honored posthumously as Prince Jing.

sentences.zh: 子頃王儀嗣。

translations.literal: His son Prince Qing, Yi, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: He was succeeded by Liu Yi, posthumously Prince Qing.

sentences.zh: 永建二年，封儀兄二人為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongjian, two elder brothers of Yi were enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 127 CE two of Liu Yi's brothers became village marquises.

sentences.zh: 儀立十七年薨，子節王豹嗣。

translations.literal: Yi, having been established seventeen years, died; his son Prince Jie, Bao, succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yi reigned seventeen years and was succeeded by Liu Bao, posthumously Prince Jie.

translations.literal: Lacuna marker in the received text.
translations.idiomatic: The manuscript shows a lacuna here.

translations.literal: (Yong)
translations.idiomatic: The next gloss supplies the syllable Yong, completing a reign title.

sentences.zh: **[元]*嘉元年，封豹兄四人為亭侯。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yuanjia, four elder brothers of Bao were enfeoffed as village marquises.
translations.idiomatic: In 151 CE four of Liu Bao's brothers received village marquisates.

sentences.zh: 豹立八年薨，子暠嗣。

translations.literal: Bao, having been established eight years, died; his son Gao succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Bao died after eight years and was succeeded by Liu Gao.

sentences.zh: 三十二年，遭黃巾賊，□國走，建安十一年國除。

translations.literal: In the thirty-second year he encountered the Yellow Turbans, abandoned his state and fled; in the eleventh year of Jian'an the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In the thirty-second year of his reign the rebellion struck; he fled his capital, and the kingdom ended in 206 CE.

sentences.zh: 濟陰悼王長

translations.literal: Prince Dao of Jiyin, Chang.
translations.idiomatic: Prince Dao of Jiyin, Liu Chang.

sentences.zh: 濟陰悼王長，永平十五年封。

translations.literal: Prince Dao of Jiyin, Chang, in the fifteenth year of Yongping was enfeoffed.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Chang received Jiyin in 72 CE.

sentences.zh: 建初四年，以東郡之離狐﹑陳留之長垣益濟陰國。

translations.literal: In the fourth year of Jianchu, with Lihu of Dong commandery and Changyuan of Chenliu they augmented the state of Jiyin.
translations.idiomatic: In 79 CE Lihu and Changyuan counties were added to his appanage.

sentences.zh: 立十三年，薨於京師，無子，國除。

translations.literal: Having been established thirteen years, he died at the capital; without a son, the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He died in the capital after thirteen years without an heir, and the line lapsed.

sentences.zh: 論曰：晏子稱「夫人生厚而用利，於是乎正德以幅之，謂之幅利」。

translations.literal: The essay says: Yanzi said, "When men are born to plenty and use it for profit, then correct virtue is used to bound it; this is called bounding profit."
translations.idiomatic: The historian observes with Yanzi that wealth must be hemmed in by virtue, like cloth by its selvage.

sentences.zh: 言人情須節以正其德，亦由布帛須幅以成其度焉。

translations.literal: It means human feeling must be moderated to correct its virtue, just as silk must have width to complete its measure.
translations.idiomatic: Human desire needs restraint the way cloth needs a measured width.

sentences.zh: [一]明帝封諸子，租歲不過二千萬，馬後為言而不得也。

translations.literal: [One] When Mingdi enfeoffed his sons, yearly rent did not exceed twenty million, and Empress Ma spoke for them yet could not obtain more.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming capped his sons' revenues at twenty million cash a year; even Empress Ma could not raise the limit.

sentences.zh: [二]賢哉！

translations.literal: [Two] How worthy!
translations.idiomatic: How worthy she was!

sentences.zh: 豈徒儉約而已乎！

translations.literal: Was it only frugality!
translations.idiomatic: Her wisdom went far beyond mere thrift.

sentences.zh: 知驕貴之無猒，嗜欲之難極也，故東京諸侯鮮有至於禍敗者也。

translations.literal: She knew that pride in nobility knows no satiety and that craving is hard to exhaust; therefore the Eastern Han princes rarely fell into disaster and ruin.
translations.idiomatic: She understood that privilege and appetite know no limit, which is why Later Han princes seldom came to ruin.

sentences.zh: 注[一]左傳雲，齊景公與晏子邶殿之邑六十，晏子不受，曰：「夫富如布帛之有幅焉，為之度使無遷也。

translations.literal: Comment one: The Zuo Tradition says that Duke Jing of Qi offered Yanzi sixty settlements in Beidian; Yanzi would not accept, saying: "Wealth is like cloth and silk having width; one makes a measure so it does not shift.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The gloss quotes Yanzi's reply to Duke Jing of Qi from the Zuo Tradition, declining lavish estates.

sentences.zh: 夫人生厚而用利，於是正德以幅之，謂之幅利。

translations.literal: When men are born to plenty and use it for profit, then correct virtue is used to bound it; this is called bounding profit.
translations.idiomatic: Wealth without moral bounds invites ruin; virtue sets the margin, Yanzi said.

sentences.zh: 過則為敗，吾不敢貪多，所謂幅也。」

translations.literal: Excess then becomes defeat; I dare not be greedy for more; this is what is called the width."
translations.idiomatic: Greed overspills the bolt of cloth; I refuse more land than my virtue can span."

sentences.zh: 注[二]東觀明紀曰：「皇子之封，皆減舊制。

translations.literal: Comment two: The Eastern View Ming Annals says: "The enfeoffment of imperial sons was all reduced from the old system.
translations.idiomatic: The Eastern Lodge account of Emperor Ming states that princely endowments were cut back from earlier norms.

sentences.zh: 嘗案輿地圖，皇后在傍，言鉅鹿﹑樂成﹑廣平各數縣，租谷百萬，帝令滿二千萬止。

translations.literal: Once he examined the map of territories with the empress at his side, speaking of Julu, Lecheng, and Guangping, each several counties, rent grain reaching a million, the emperor ordered that twenty million be the ceiling.
translations.idiomatic: Consulting the territorial register with Empress Ma at his side, he capped the revenues of Julu, Lecheng, and Guangping at twenty million bushels of grain.

sentences.zh: 諸小王皆當略與楚﹑淮陽相比，什減三四。

translations.literal: The various younger princes should on the whole compare to Chu and Huaiyang, reduced by three or four tenths.
translations.idiomatic: Younger princes were to receive roughly thirty or forty percent less than the great kingdoms of Chu and Huaiyang.

sentences.zh: 『我子不當與先帝子等』者也。」

translations.literal: It says: "My sons must not be made equal to the late emperor's sons."
translations.idiomatic: So ran his verdict: "My sons shall not rival my father's sons in income."

sentences.zh: 贊曰：孝明傳胤，維城八國。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Filial Ming handed down heirs; eight states were walls for the realm.
translations.idiomatic: The summation praises Emperor Ming for planting eight princely houses as bulwarks of the dynasty.

sentences.zh: 陳敬嚴重，彭城厚德。

translations.literal: Chen was reverend and weighty; Pengcheng was rich in virtue.
translations.idiomatic: Chen stood for dignity; Pengcheng for steadfast goodness.

sentences.zh: 下邳嬰痾，梁節邪惑。

translations.literal: Xiapi suffered lingering illness; Liang Jie was deluded by heterodoxy.
translations.idiomatic: Xiapi was crippled by chronic disease; Prince Jie of Liang fell to sorcery and sedition.

sentences.zh: 三藩夙齡，[一]黨惟荒忒。

translations.literal: Three fiefs at tender age; [one] Dang alone was wildly reckless.
translations.idiomatic: Three kingdoms lost their princes in youth; only Liu Dang ran to extremes of depravity.

sentences.zh: 注[一]謂千乘﹑淮陽﹑濟陰並早歿也。

translations.literal: Comment one: It means Qiansheng, Huaiyang, and Jiyin all died young.
translations.idiomatic: Note 1: The three early deaths are Qiansheng, Huaiyang, and Jiyin.

sentences.zh: 校勘記

translations.literal: Collation notes
translations.idiomatic: Textual collation

sentences.zh: 一六六七頁四行本書謂東觀記也按：「東」原斗「雲」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1667 line 4: this book says it refers to the Eastern Lodge Annals. Note: the graph for east was miscopied as the graph for cloud; corrected from the Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1667 line 4: the work cited is the Eastern Lodge Annals; a copyist's error substituting "cloud" for "east" is emended from the Ji and Palace recensions.

sentences.zh: 一六六七頁八行與諸儒講論於白虎殿按：張森楷校勘記謂何焯云「殿」疑作「觀」。

translations.literal: Page 1667 line 8: debating with scholars in the White Tiger Hall. Note: Zhang Senkai's collation cites He Zhuo's suggestion that hall should read hall of observation.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1667 line 8: some scholars suspect the venue should read White Tiger Hall of Literature rather than Hall.

sentences.zh: 一六七０頁八行多為丹*

translations.literal: Page 1670 line 8: many became Dan (lacuna in received text).
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1670 line 8: the manuscript breaks after the syllable dan.

translations.literal: (Yang)
translations.idiomatic: The gloss supplies the syllable yang in parentheses.

sentences.zh: **[陵]*兵據汲本、殿本改。

translations.literal: The graph for troops emended per Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: The word for soldiers is corrected from the Ji and Palace editions.

sentences.zh: 按：殿本考證謂「陵」監本誤作「陽」，今改正。

translations.literal: Note: the Palace edition critical note says the Palace block miswrote ridge as yang; now corrected.
translations.idiomatic: The Palace edition notes that the Directorate edition wrongly wrote "yang" for "ling"; the text is amended.

sentences.zh: 一六七一頁五行恭子男丁前*[妻]*物故按：王先謙謂今本東觀記「前」下有「妻」字，是也。

translations.literal: Page 1671 line 5: Gong's son Ding's former wife deceased. Note: Wang Xianqian says the current Eastern Lodge text correctly has the word wife after former.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1671 line 5: Wang Xianqian observes that the Eastern Lodge Annals insert the word for wife after former, which is correct.

sentences.zh: 下又引東觀記，雲丁為魯陽鄉侯，則是丁未物故，而物故者乃其妻也。

translations.literal: Below it again cites the Eastern Lodge, saying Ding was Marquis of Luyang village, so Ding had not died; the one who died was his wife.
translations.idiomatic: A later passage makes Ding marquis of Luyang village, showing Ding was alive and the deceased party was his wife.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The missing word is supplied on that evidence.

sentences.zh: 一六七一頁一二行封定兄弟九人皆為亭侯按：校補引錢大昭說，謂據東觀記當作「兄弟八人」。

translations.literal: Page 1671 line 12: enfeoffed Ding's nine brothers as village marquises. Note: the collation supplement cites Qian Dazhao: per the Eastern Lodge it should read eight brothers.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1671 line 12: Qian Dazhao argues the Eastern Lodge reads eight brothers, not nine.

sentences.zh: 一六七二頁七行嫁為男子章初妻按：「初」原斗「諸」，逕據汲本、殿本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1672 line 7: married as wife of the commoner Zhang Chu. Note: Chu was originally miscopied as various; corrected from Ji and Palace editions.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1672 line 7: the groom's name Chu was corrupted to a graph meaning various; emended from Ji and Palace.

sentences.zh: 一六七三頁四行修縣*

translations.literal: Page 1673 line 4: Xiu County (lacuna marker).
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1673 line 4: lacuna after the county name Xiu.

translations.literal: (and)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical conjunction and in the lacuna.

translations.literal: Namely Tiao County (lacuna marker).
translations.idiomatic: The gloss identifies Tiao County with manuscript damage.

translations.literal: (both)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical both.

sentences.zh: *屬勃海集解引沉欽韓說，謂注「及」當為「即」，又衍一「皆」字。

translations.literal: Belonged to Bohai. Collected glosses cite Shen Qinhan: the commentary graph and should read namely, and one redundant graph both should be dropped.
translations.idiomatic: Shen Qinhan argues the commentary should read namely instead of and and should omit an intrusive both.

sentences.zh: 今按：漢書地理志作「修」，景帝紀、周亞夫傳作「條」，師古曰「修音條」，是修縣即條縣也，沉說是，今據改。

translations.literal: Now checked: the Han shu treatise on geography writes Xiu; Emperor Jing's annals and Zhou Yafu's biography write Tiao; Yan Shigu says Xiu is read like Tiao; therefore Xiu County is Tiao County; Shen is right; now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: Geographical sources equate Xiu and Tiao; Yan Shigu equates the readings; Shen's emendation is adopted.

sentences.zh: 一六七三頁八行毆擊吏人按：「毆」原斗「驅」，逕據集解本改正。

translations.literal: Page 1673 line 8: striking clerks and commoners. Note: strike was originally miscopied as drive; corrected from the Collected Glosses edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1673 line 8: the verb for beating was corrupted to drive; fixed from the critical edition.

sentences.zh: 一六七四頁二行尚書侍郎冷宏按：汲本「冷」作「泠」。

translations.literal: Page 1674 line 2: Gentleman of the Secretariat Leng Hong. Note: Ji edition writes Ling for the surname.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1674 line 2: the Ji edition spells the minister's surname Ling instead of Leng.

sentences.zh: 一六七四頁七行子續立按：汲本「續」作「績」。

translations.literal: Page 1674 line 7: son Xu succeeded. Note: Ji edition writes merit graph instead of continue.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1674 line 7: the heir's name appears as Xu in one edition and a homophone in the Ji text.

sentences.zh: 一六七四頁一四行在今豪州按：殿本「豪」作「濠」。

translations.literal: Page 1674 line 14: today in Haozhou. Note: Palace edition writes Moatzhou instead of Haozhou.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1674 line 14: the Palace edition uses the standard graph for the Chuzhou placename.

sentences.zh: 一六七六頁一行□今許州郾陵縣也按：「□」汲本作「鄢」，殿本作「郾」。

translations.literal: Page 1676 line 1: lacuna, today Yanling County in Xuzhou. Note: lacuna filled as Yan in Ji edition and as swallow graph in Palace edition.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1676 line 1: the missing county name is supplied as Yanling from parallel editions.

sentences.zh: 集解引惠棟說，謂正文之「郾」，亦當依注作「鄢」。

translations.literal: Collected glosses cite Hui Dong: the main text swallow graph should likewise follow the commentary and read Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Hui Dong argues the main-text placename should match the commentary form.

sentences.zh: 又引錢大昕說，謂郡國志「郾」作「□」，此字亦誤，當為「鄢」。

translations.literal: It also cites Qian Daxin: the treatise on commanderies writes swallow graph as lacuna; that graph is also wrong and should be Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Daxin notes the treatise variant and prefers the reading Yan.

sentences.zh: 校補謂案光武紀「三月，光武別與諸將徇昆陽、定陵、郾，皆下之」。

translations.literal: The collation supplement says: checking Emperor Guangwu's annals for the third month, Guangwu separately with various generals took Kunyang, Dingling, and swallow graph, all subduing them.
translations.idiomatic: The supplement cites Guangwu's campaign list to settle the orthography.

sentences.zh: 彼注云「郾，今豫州郾城縣也」。

translations.literal: That commentary note says swallow graph is today Yancheng County in Yuzhou.
translations.idiomatic: Li Xian's note identifies the county with modern Yancheng in Henan.

sentences.zh: 章懷既釋郾為豫州之郾城，則此雲許州郾陵，當然是「鄢」非「郾」，不獨殿本注作「郾」誤，各本正文作「郾」皆誤矣。

translations.literal: Since Zhanghuai already glossed swallow graph as Yancheng in Yuzhou, this saying Xuzhou Yanling must mean Yan and not swallow graph; not only is the Palace commentary swallow graph wrong, every main-text swallow graph is wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The commentator concludes the text must mean Yanling near Xuchang, not the Yancheng form.

sentences.zh: 惟「鄢」之作「□」，似不應遽指為誤。

translations.literal: Only for Yan written as lacuna, it seems one should not hastily call it an error.
translations.idiomatic: A lacuna standing for Yan need not be presumed erroneous.

sentences.zh: 鄢陵前、續志均屬穎川郡，鄢前志屬陳留郡，續志屬梁國，字則前志均作「傿」，續志均作「□」，更無作「鄢」者，如以為誤，則前志亦誤矣。

translations.literal: Yanling in former and latter treatises both belonged to Yingchuan commandery; Yan in the former treatise belonged to Chenliu commandery, in the latter to Liang state; the characters in the former treatise both write borrowed graph, in the latter both lacuna; moreover none write Yan; if that is deemed error, then the former treatise is also wrong.
translations.idiomatic: The geographical registers use several variant graphs for these adjacent counties; emending one forces a chain of further changes.

sentences.zh: 一六七六頁一一行而令陛下為臣收污天下按：集解引顧炎武說，謂「收污」袁宏紀作「收恥」，通鑒作「受污」。

translations.literal: Page 1676 line 11: and caused Your Majesty to gather vileness for your subject throughout the realm. Note: Collected glosses cite Gu Yanwu: gather vileness Yuan Hong annals reads gather shame; Comprehensive Mirror reads receive vileness.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1676 line 11: Gu Yanwu notes parallel texts read shame or receive disgrace instead of gather vileness.

sentences.zh: 一六七六頁一五行誠無心面目以凶惡復居大宮按：集解引蘇輿說，謂「心」字疑衍。

translations.literal: Page 1676 line 15: truly has no heart or face to dwell again in a great palace. Note: Collected glosses cite Su Yu: the word heart may be redundant.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1676 line 15: Su Yu suspects the word for heart is a dittograph.

sentences.zh: 一六七七頁一行假臣小善之路殿本「小」作「遷」。

translations.literal: Page 1677 line 1: lend a path for your subject's small good. Palace edition writes shift instead of small.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1677 line 1: the Palace edition reads shift where others read small.

sentences.zh: 今按：袁紀亦作「小」。

translations.literal: Now checked: Yuan annals also writes small.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan Hong's annals agree on small.

sentences.zh: 一六七七頁五行志匪由*

translations.literal: Page 1677 line 5: intent not from lacuna.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1677 line 5: lacuna in the phrase about intent.

translations.literal: (from)
translations.idiomatic: Parenthetical from.

sentences.zh: **[王]*咎在彼小子校補引柳從辰說，謂「於」字系「王」字之鬥，「咎」字屬下讀。

translations.literal: The blame lies with those petty fellows. Collation supplement cites Liu Congchen: the graph from is a corruption of king; blame attaches to the next sentence.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Congchen argues the lacuna should read king and the sentence break should follow blame.

sentences.zh: 又謂「於」當作「王」，錢大昭已有是說。

translations.literal: It also says from should be king; Qian Dazhao already had that view.
translations.idiomatic: Qian Dazhao had already proposed the same emendation.

sentences.zh: 今據改。

translations.literal: Now emended accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The text is updated per that argument.

sentences.zh: 一六七八頁五行永平*[十]*五年封常山王校補引錢大昭說，謂「五年」當作「十五年」，脫「十」字。

translations.literal: Page 1678 line 5: fifteenth year of Yongping enfeoffed as Prince of Changshan. Collation supplement cites Qian Dazhao: five years should read fifteen years, missing the ten graph.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1678 line 5: Qian Dazhao restores the tens digit to the Yongping date.

sentences.zh: 今據補。

translations.literal: Now supplemented accordingly.
translations.idiomatic: The missing ten is supplied.

sentences.zh: 一六七八頁五行以汝南之新安西華益淮陽國按：集解引錢大昕說，謂汝南郡無新安縣，疑「新陽」之鬥。

translations.literal: Page 1678 line 5: with Xin'an and Xihua of Runan they augmented Huaiyang state. Note: Collected glosses cite Qian Daxin: Runan commandery has no Xin'an County; suspect a corruption of Xinyang.
translations.idiomatic: Collation at page 1678 line 5: Qian Daxin doubts Xin'an exists in Runan and proposes Xinyang instead.
